title: Seed cake
subtitle: A buttery Victorian sponge flavoured with caraway
excerpt: A traditional British loaf cake made with caraway seeds and a creamed butter-and-sugar batter. The caraway gives a faint anise note that is unusual in a sweet cake but entirely characteristic of this old British recipe. Keeps well for five days wrapped.
sourceNotes: Seed cake is one of the oldest British baked goods, documented from at least the eighteenth century. The recipe appears in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136) and in Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747, Project Gutenberg). Caraway as a flavouring for sweet cakes is described in multiple pre-1928 British household baking guides and cookery columns available through the British Newspaper Archive.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze wrapped individually. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves by the next day. Keeps wrapped at room temperature for up to 5 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 175°C. Low and slow prevents the crust from darkening before the centre is set.

glossaryTerms.definition: The standard method for a butter cake: beat softened butter and sugar together until pale and fluffy, then add eggs one at a time, then fold in the dry ingredients. The beating incorporates air into the fat and sugar, which expands in the oven to leaven the cake. Under-creaming produces a dense crumb; over-mixing once the flour is added develops gluten and toughens the texture. Stop folding as soon as no streaks of flour remain.

content.text: Seed cake is a British classic: a butter sponge flavoured with caraway seeds rather than vanilla or chocolate. The caraway gives a clean anise note that cuts through the richness of the buttery crumb. The method is a straightforward
: butter and sugar beaten together, then eggs, then flour. One 900 g loaf tin, ready in about an hour.

Heat the oven to 165°C fan (175°C conventional). Grease and line the loaf tin with baking paper. Beat the softened butter and sugar together in a large bowl for 3 to 4 minutes, until pale and noticeably fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each.

Sift the flour, baking powder, and salt into the bowl. Add the caraway seeds and milk. Fold gently with a large spoon or spatula until no streaks of flour remain; the batter will be thick. Scrape into the tin and level the surface.

Bake for 50 to 55 minutes, until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean and the top is golden brown. If the surface colours quickly, cover loosely with foil after 30 minutes. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool completely.
